experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I have no concrete role information yet; as a Good Servant on round 1, the standard play is usually to approve an initial 2-person team that includes the leader and no suspicious history exists. Approving reveals little and helps generate quest-result information, while rejecting the very first neutral team gives up tempo without evidence.
Player 1: I have no concrete role information yet; as a good Servant on round 1, a standard 2-person opening team including the leader and me is reasonable. Approving preserves tempo and reveals quest outcome information without giving evil much extra leverage. Rejecting without evidence is less useful this early.
Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so team [0,1] contains no evil and would guarantee a successful first quest if approved. As evil in the voting phase, the optimal play is usually to avoid obvious anti-town behavior early while still trying to keep non-evil teams off quests; rejecting a clean team also helps prevent town from gaining a trusted success pair. My vote alone does not reveal much on round 1 since early rejections are common, and rejecting slightly helps evil by pushing for a different team that may include me or 4.
Player 3: I know players 2 and 4 are Evil, so proposed team [0,1] contains no known Evil and is excellent for Good on quest 1. As Merlin, I want early successful teams without revealing too much; approving a clean team is optimal and does not strongly expose my knowledge because many players would also approve an initial 2-person team excluding obvious concerns. My vote supports a likely pass and helps Good establish trustworthy players.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,3 are good-aligned except one may be Merlin/Percival. The proposed team [0,1] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and give good an early clean result. In team voting as evil, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams, especially on quest 1, to push for a team containing evil later and create ambiguity. My reject reveals little because rejecting an all-good opening team is common and not strongly incriminating.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], so both 0 and 1 look somewhat more trustworthy, though one could still be evil and choose to pass. Adding 2 on Quest 2 is a standard informative expansion and not especially suspicious. As a good Servant, approving a reasonable team that includes two players from a successful quest is optimal. My vote reveals I support evidence-based continuity rather than random disruption, which helps good more than evil here.
Player 1: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,1], which makes 0 and 1 somewhat more trustworthy, though one success does not clear anyone fully. Adding 2 to [0,1] is a reasonable, information-light good team for quest 2, and as a Servant I generally want to approve solid teams that preserve successful members. Approving reveals consistency with backing a team built from the only successful core so far and does not meaningfully hurt Good.
Player 2: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 0 and 1 were on the successful first quest, so at least one of them is likely good, though as evil I mainly want to avoid giving good too much confidence while creating chances to control future quests. Current phase is team voting on [0,1,2], which includes me and excludes my teammate 4. As evil, approving gets an evil player onto the quest, creating an opportunity to fail quest 2 and tie the game, though it may implicate me. Rejecting gives up immediate sabotage potential. The information revealed by approving is acceptable because a fail here is strong for evil tempo, and if I choose pass later I can preserve cover. Optimal vote is approve.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know players 2 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1,2] includes confirmed Evil player 2, so this team is extremely likely to fail and should be rejected. As Merlin, I should avoid obviously outing my knowledge too much, but voting reject on a team containing an Evil is standard and helps Good prevent a bad quest outcome.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, while 0 and 1 are currently unconfirmed but look somewhat trusted because their two-person quest succeeded. This proposed team includes my teammate 2, giving evil a chance to fail quest 2, but approving also puts exactly one evil on a 3-person team, which can be useful without strongly exposing both evils. As evil/Assassin in team voting, I want teams with evil on them to pass and teams without evil to fail; my vote itself reveals limited information because approving this team is natural for both some good players and for me.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Two successful quests included players 0 and 1 on quest 1, then 0,1,2 on quest 2, so players 0,1,2 all have some good evidence; at least none of them failed while on mission. As a Servant on team voting for quest 3 in 5-player Avalon, a 2-person team of 0 and 2 is attractive because both have been on successful missions and excluding untested 3/4 reduces risk. Approving supports a high-probability good team and reveals little harmful information.
Player 1: Two successful quests with teams [0,1] and [0,1,2] mean 0,1,2 all look good; at least neither quest included a fail. As a Servant, approving a team of [0,2] is reasonable because both have been on successful teams and this continues testing among trusted players. Rejecting would withhold a likely good team and gives little extra info on round 1.
Player 2: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 0 and 1 have both been on successful teams, and I was also on the last successful team. As evil, with us down 0-2, I need chances to get an evil player onto quests and create doubt without overexposing myself. This is a team vote on my own proposed team [0,2]. Approving keeps me looking consistent and gets me onto the quest, where I can choose fail if it meaningfully helps evil. Voting against my own team would be very suspicious and reveal unhelpful information.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know player 2 is Evil. The proposed team [0,2] contains a known Evil, so from Good's perspective this team should be rejected to avoid risking a fail on quest 3. Voting reject is also consistent with protecting information: I should oppose bad teams without being overly explicit, and rejecting a 2-player team with a known suspicious member helps Good more than it hurts.
Player 4: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 succeeded with team [0,1,2], meaning 2 chose to pass, likely to preserve cover while evil is down 0-2. Current proposed team [0,2] puts exactly one evil on a 2-person team, which gives us a chance to fail Quest 3 if approved. As evil, the optimal play is to approve teams containing an evil player, especially when we need to start catching up. My approve vote is slightly suspicious given prior successes, but rejecting a team with my teammate would hurt evil more than the minor info leak helps good.

Player 2: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,3 are good. Two quests have already succeeded with zero fails, and evil is down 0-2, so we need to start creating failures. But if I pass again, good reaches 3 successes and wins before assassination. As evil on quest 3, the optimal play is to sabotage now despite the exposure, because not failing loses the game instantly. The information revealed hurts my cover but preserving any chance for evil requires a fail.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Good Servant. From results: Quest1 [0,1] success means both likely good or evil chose pass. Quest2 [0,1,2] success then Quest3 [0,2] failed with one fail strongly implicates 2 as evil, since 0 was on all three and if 0 were evil they could have failed earlier; also Quest2 succeeding with 2 suggests 2 hid there. So safest known-good core is 0 and 1, with 2 suspicious. Between 3 and 4, leader 3 includes self and excludes 2, which is consistent with good play. On team voting, approving a team of [0,1,3] tests 3 while keeping out the main suspect 2. Approving reveals useful info and is low risk compared with including 2.
Player 1: Player 2 was on the failed 2-person quest with Player 0, so at least one of {0,2} is evil. Since Quests 1 and 2 with teams [0,1] and [0,1,2] both succeeded, 1 is good (me) and it is more suspicious on 2 than 0 because evil can choose to pass early, but on a 2-person failed quest one evil was enough to fail. Team [0,1,3] excludes the most suspicious player 2 and keeps me plus an untested 3. As Good, approving a team that avoids the prime suspect and includes a confirmed good (me) is optimal. Approving also gives more information on 3 while preserving a plausible good team for quest 4.
Player 2: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Quest 3 failed on team [0,2], implicating me to observant players, so excluding me from this team may make it look safer. But as evil, I want a good-only team rejected if possible because this proposed team [0,1,3] likely contains all good players and would succeed, giving Good their third success and the game. Voting reject is optimal to prevent an immediate Good win and does not reveal much since I can justify suspicion around 0/1/3 alignments or general caution on quest 4.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 2 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,3] excludes both known evil players, so it is a strong all-good team candidate for a 3-person quest. On team voting, optimal good play is to approve clearly good teams. Approving also helps signal confidence in a clean team without directly exposing Merlin too much, since as leader I already proposed it.
Player 4: I know player 2 is evil with me. Quest 3 team [0,2] failed with exactly one fail, strongly implicating 2 or 0; since I know 2 is evil, 0 now looks good from others' perspective but may still be suspected. Proposed team [0,1,3] excludes both evils, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and put Good to match point at 3-1, which is bad for evil. As Assassin on team voting, optimal play is to reject any all-good team to try to force a different proposal that includes an evil player. My vote reveals little because rejecting a team after a failed quest is normal and can align with suspicion on 0 or desire for caution, so it does not strongly expose me.
